title: Administrative services | NHSScotland Careers
content: Home Explore careers Administrative services Administrative services Administrative services make up one of the largest staff groups in the NHSScotland workforce. The NHS relies on people working in administrative roles to make sure healthcare services run efficiently and smoothly every day. Administrative assistant Communications officer Digital delivery manager Finance assistant Health economist HR assistant Information analyst Information security officer IT support engineer Project officer Software engineer Strategic planner Ward clerk Library and knowledge services assistant Senior library and knowledge services assistant Public health librarian Learning for Career Success Learning for Career Success is an online career planning resource for the support workforce. It will help you find work-based learning opportunities to enhance your career. title: Allied health professions | NHSScotland Careers
content: Home Explore careers Allied health professions You can be an allied health professional Allied health professionals (AHPs) use their unique skills and knowledge to improve people's lives. You may already have heard of a physiotherapist, a dietitian, or a paramedic. Let's explore all the AHP job roles in the NHSScotland workforce. Who are AHPs? Allied health professionals are the third largest clinical workforce in NHSScotland. AHPs assess, diagnose, and treat people of all ages to enhance their health, wellbeing, and quality of life. AHPs also signpost people to other services to help them access additional care and support they may need. Watch our video to learn more about all 14 AHP careers. Art therapist Diagnostic radiographer Dietitian Dramatherapist Music therapist Occupational therapist Orthoptist Orthotist Paramedic Physiotherapist Podiatrist Prosthetist Speech and language therapist Therapeutic radiographer AHP practice educator Innovative and diverse roles You could be an AHP working in clinical practice in a wide range of environments, including community settings, GP practices, hospitals, patients' homes, prison services, and schools. AHPs also work in: evidence, research, and development to inform practice and improve services roles that facilitate learning to support the education and development of others leadership roles in leading teams and managing services There are so many possibilities for AHPs in the NHS. Help shape the future of AHP education in Scotland Discover the benefits of becoming an AHP practice educator or contributing to placement delivery. Become an AHP practice educator Returning to an AHP career Are you a qualified AHP who wants to return to a career in health and social care? Find out how to re-register with the Health and Care Professions Council and return to practice. Start the return to practice process Help with recruitment We'll guide you through the recruitment process, from applying online to interview preparation. Help with recruitment AHP career guide Our new AHP career guide tells you all you need to know about our 14 allied health professions. Discover more

title: Ambulance services | NHSScotland Careers
content: Home Explore careers Ambulance services Ambulance services The Scottish Ambulance Service ensures an immediate response with the most appropriate skills and equipment is provided to 999 emergency calls to save lives. This could involve an ambulance, rapid response car, motorcycle, bicycle or air ambulance. The ambulance transport team drives non-emergency patients to hospitals for appointments or routine admissions, and to their home following discharge from hospital. Ambulance care assistant Ambulance technician Emergency call handler Paramedic Explore careers Discover the skills and qualifications you’ll need for each role and what the work will be like. title: NHS Career Choices Overview
content: NHS Scotland offers a wide range of career options across several key areas. Main NHS career groups include Medical Careers, Nursing and Midwifery, Allied Health Professions, Healthcare Science, Ambulance Services, Support Services, and Administration and Management. These roles support patient care and the delivery of healthcare services across Scotland.

title: Dental | NHSScotland Careers
content: Home Explore careers Dental Dental The dental team supports dentists to provide oral health services to people, including the treatment of dental and oral disease, dental irregularities and injuries. Dental nurse Dental technician Dental therapist Dentist Explore careers Discover the skills and qualifications you’ll need for each role and what the work will be like. Explore careers Help with recruitment We'll guide you through the recruitment process, from applying online to interview preparation. Help with recruitment

title: Health play services | NHSScotland Careers
content: Home Explore careers Health play services Health play services Health play services use therapeutic play to help children and young people, their families, and carers have a positive experience of healthcare. It aids in the understanding of illness and treatment, reduces anxiety, and provides relaxation in unfamiliar surroundings. What do health play services do? Health play specialists can guide children through role-play and therapeutic play to help them: express feelings interact with medical equipment relieve boredom become comfortable in unfamiliar surroundings build confidence within hospital and clinical staff Health play specialists need a foundation degree in Healthcare Play Specialism. Health play assistants also work within the play team and have childcare qualifications. Watch this video produced by NHS Lothian to find out more about what play services do to support and care for children in hospital. Health play specialist Health play assistant Explore careers Discover the skills and qualifications you’ll need for each role and what the work will be like. Explore careers Pay and benefits Our pay and benefits structure aims to attract, retain, and develop a skilled workforce. Pay and benefits

title: Healthcare science | NHSScotland Careers
content: Home Explore careers Healthcare science Healthcare science The healthcare science workforce is involved in 80% of diagnostic decisions in patient care. They’re at the heart of improving healthcare and advancing scientific development through technology and innovation. Who are healthcare scientists? Did you know that there are more than 50 different healthcare science careers? Healthcare scientists use science, data, product design, and engineering to: prevent, diagnose, monitor, and treat medical conditions design and develop custom medical devices commission and maintain medical equipment make, improve, and adapt assistive technology There are many routes to a rewarding healthcare science career, including Modern Apprenticeships, entry-level support roles, and training programmes. Engineering sciences Health informatics sciences Laboratory sciences Physical and imaging sciences Physiological sciences A career for you in healthcare science Ready to discover some of the healthcare science careers you can choose in the NHS? Check out our new career guide! Download now Help with recruitment We'll guide you through the recruitment process, from applying online to interview preparation. Help with recruitment

content: Home Explore careers Healthcare support workers Healthcare support workers Healthcare support workers assist nurses, therapists, midwives, hospital doctors and other healthcare professionals in delivering high-quality, person-centred care. title: Medical associate professions | NHSScotland Careers
content: Home Explore careers Medical associate professions Medical associate professions Medical associate professions (MAPs) include physician associates, anaesthesia associates, and surgical care practitioners. They are trained healthcare professionals who provide patient care under supervision. MAPs work in multidisciplinary teams with doctors, nurses, and other healthcare workers. Anaesthesia associate Physician associate Explore careers Discover the skills and qualifications you’ll need for each role and what the work will be like. title: Medicine | NHSScotland Careers
content: Home Explore careers Medicine Medicine Choose a career in medicine where you can make a positive impact on people's lives. With over 65 different specialties, medicine offers a rewarding and purpose-driven career path. You can be a doctor in the NHS Did you know that in Scotland, there are 5 medical schools where you can study medicine? After your undergraduate degree, you'll receive world-class postgraduate training in teaching hospitals and community healthcare settings to become a doctor. If you don't have the qualifications for medical school, there are access routes for school leavers and adults returning to education. Anaesthetist General practitioner Ophthalmologist Surgeon NHSScotland Careers blog Our blog includes how-to guides, case studies, and career resources. Discover more Help with recruitment We'll guide you through the recruitment process, from applying online to interview preparation. Help with recruitment

title: Midwifery | NHSScotland Careers
content: Home Explore careers Midwifery Midwifery Midwives and health visitors help prepare new parents for parenthood by supporting them in making lifestyle changes to benefit their future family life. Being a midwife covers much more than delivering babies, although that’s a crucial part of the job. If you would like to support mothers during pregnancy and ensure the best quality of care for babies at the earliest stages of life, this could be the career for you. Midwife Health visitor Explore careers Discover the skills and qualifications you’ll need for each role and what the work will be like. Explore careers Help with recruitment We'll guide you through the recruitment process, from applying online to interview preparation. Help with recruitment

title: NHS 24 service delivery | NHSScotland Careers
content: Home Explore careers NHS 24 service delivery NHS 24 service delivery NHS 24 is a patient-focused service that operates 24 hours a day, 365 days a year. It provides advice and assistance to people over the phone and online, helping them get the right care in the right place. NHS 24 unscheduled care NHS 24's unscheduled care service was established in 2004. It receives 1.5 million calls every year from people across Scotland on the free 111 phone number. All calls are evaluated by a triage team and directed to a healthcare professional, where appropriate. In a life or limb-threatening emergency, calls are transferred to the Scottish Ambulance Service. Discover some of the jobs available at NHS 24. Call handler Psychological wellbeing practitioner Pay and benefits Find out about our flexible working policies, pay rates, pensions, health and wellbeing initiatives, and career development support. Learn more about pay and benefits NHSScotland Careers blog Our blog features how-to guides, case studies, and resources for career planning, researching roles, and discovering what it's like to work in the NHS. Discover more

title: Nursing | NHSScotland Careers
content: Home Explore careers Nursing Nursing The key role of a nurse is to deliver person-centred, safe and effective care. Nurses help patients understand information about their diagnoses and treatment. They also respond to each patient’s physical, clinical, and emotional needs. Areas of nursing practice In the UK, there are 4 areas of practice. These are: adult nursing children’s nursing learning disabilities nursing mental health nursing Whichever route you take into nursing, you’ll study a degree programme in your chosen practice area. Adult nurse Children's nurse Learning disability nurse Mental health nurse Operating department practitioner Prison nurse Explore careers Discover the skills and qualifications you’ll need for each role and what the work will be like. Explore careers Help with recruitment We'll guide you through the recruitment process, from applying online to interview preparation. Help with recruitment

title: Optometry | NHSScotland Careers
content: Home Explore careers Optometry Optometry Working in vision care offers many rewarding career opportunities. As an optometrist, you'll examine a person's eyes to make sure they get the right optical treatment and access to healthcare services. As a dispensing optician, you'll provide advice and supply and fit glasses suitable for a person's lifestyle and vocational needs. Dispensing optician Optometrist Help with recruitment We'll guide you through the recruitment process, from applying online to interview preparation. Help with recruitment NHSScotland Careers blog Our blog includes how-to guides, case studies, and career resources. Discover more

title: Pharmacy | NHSScotland Careers
content: Home Explore careers Pharmacy Pharmacy Do you enjoy helping and supporting others? A career in pharmacy services could be for you if you like science subjects like Biology and Chemistry. You'll also need good communication skills to work with patients and the public. Make a difference with a career in pharmacy services In hospitals, pharmacy staff provide advice and support to patients on how to take their medicines. They identify the medicines patients were taking before hospital admission and make sure they are still appropriate when the patient returns home. Pharmacy staff work in primary care too. They see patients who need help with their medicines. They also treat people with long-term conditions, such as asthma and diabetes. Community pharmacies are where people can buy over-the-counter products for coughs, aches, and pains. They can also get their prescriptions dispensed and receive lifestyle advice for better health. Pharmacist Pharmacy support worker Pharmacy technician Explore careers Discover the skills and qualifications you’ll need for each role and what the work will be like. Explore careers Help with recruitment We'll guide you through the recruitment process, from applying online to interview preparation. Help with recruitment

title: Psychology | NHSScotland Careers
content: Home Explore careers Psychology Psychology Psychology is the study of how people think, feel and behave. Through specialist techniques, such as psychometric testing and observation, psychologists investigate the causes of behaviour and the mental processes involved. Psychologists work closely with patients to manage and treat their condition to improve mental health and wellbeing. Assistant psychologist Clinical associate in applied psychology Clinical psychologist Counselling psychologist Forensic psychologist Health psychologist Neuropsychologist Explore careers Discover the skills and qualifications you’ll need for each role and what the work will be like.
